question: Tell me about yourself.

stories.title: Grambling CS foundation
stories.situation: I’m Emmanuel Acheampong. I’m a Computer Science major at Grambling State University, graduating May 2027. I’ve been on Dean’s List in ’24 and ’25, I’m on a Presidential Scholarship, and I won gold in the Vanda African Math Olympiad back in 2018. Coursework-wise I’ve done OOP, software engineering, data structures and algorithms, and operating systems.
stories.task: What I’m trying to show in interviews is that I don’t just do homework—I ship.
stories.action: That’s why I paired school with internships at Geaux and Hubtel and bigger projects like MedDiagnose, VisualizeMyAlgorithm, and a mini compiler. I like when the same DSA and systems ideas show up in something users actually touch.
stories.result: So when I build something, I’m usually thinking about tests, traceability, and whether we can measure the impact—not just “does it compile.”

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation
stories.situation: So at Geaux I was an Associate SWE Intern, remote, summer 2025. Engineering and content both cared about “who changed what” for podcasts and media, but the truth was spread across a bunch of backend event sources that didn’t always line up.
stories.task: I needed to ship a Change History view in TypeScript and SCSS that actually helped people trace edits—without trying to boil the ocean on v1.
stories.action: What I did was get both sides in one room, stack-rank the event sources by how much traceability we’d get per unit of work, then ship in phases. I surfaced the highest-value protobuf-backed events first and tightened integration tests on the read path so we weren’t guessing in prod.
stories.result: We ended up with much clearer traceability end to end, and because we phased it, releases stayed predictable instead of turning into a never-ending scope creep thing.

stories.title: Kafka/MQTT/Postgres pipeline
stories.situation: I interned at Hubtel in Ghana in 2023. The business was super transaction-heavy, so system events were noisy and downtime was real money—and real trust.
stories.task: I needed to help the team catch problems faster without destabilizing the payment workflows everyone depended on.
stories.action: I worked on a real-time pipeline with Kafka Connect, MQTT, and Postgres—basically following events from broker to sink, using integration tests and metrics when something looked off instead of guessing.
stories.result: We got downtime detection time down by something like 40%, and payments felt more available because we weren’t always firefighting blind.

question: Tell me about a time you had a disagreement with your manager.

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation

stories.result: We ended up with much clearer traceability end to end, and because we phased it, releases stayed predictable instead of turning into a never-ending scope creep thing.

stories.title: Spring Boot test expansion
stories.situation: A lot of the scary failures weren’t “unit test red”—they were integration-shaped: database, messaging, HTTP handoffs. That’s normal for payment-adjacent services.
stories.task: I needed to raise confidence in the backend paths that touched money.
stories.action: I wrote more than twenty Spring Boot tests—JUnit plus Postman collections—and I focused on transaction boundaries and the ways things actually break, not just bumping coverage for the number.
stories.result: In the areas we targeted, reliability improved a ton—on the order of 80%—and we caught regressions before they hit prod way more often.

stories.title: Cross-officer conflict
stories.situation: Officers argued about hackathons vs corporate events vs K-12 outreach. Everyone had a pet priority.
stories.task: I needed to turn that into a plan we could execute instead of debating forever.
stories.action: I ran quick surveys, looked at what people actually showed up for, and mapped sponsor commitments to specific events so we weren’t overpromising.
stories.result: Once priorities were evidence-based, execution got easier and the drama cooled down.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: I’m in ColorStack, and their hackathons pull serious competition—Winter 2024 had on the order of a thousand people worldwide.
stories.task: We had to ship something demoable on a tight clock.
stories.action: Our team cut scope to an MVP early, split frontend and backend ownership clearly, and rehearsed the demo so we weren’t doing live surgery on stage.
stories.result: We won Winter 2024, which was a good proof that I can execute when time is not on your side.

question: Tell me about a time you failed. How did you deal with the situation?

stories.title: ML + clinical translation
stories.situation: Clinicians don’t think in “model accuracy” the way engineers do—they care about when the score is misleading, when to ignore it, and how it fits into a busy shift.
stories.task: I needed the ML to be helpful without pretending it replaces judgment.
stories.action: I translated what the model was doing into plain language in the UI, documented uncertainty, and made the human decision points obvious instead of hidden.
stories.result: That kept trust. Automation still helped with routing, alerts, and eligibility prep—just in a way people could defend in a real hospital.

stories.title: Correctness first
stories.situation: Early on I moved fast and added features, and little parser bugs turned into weird IR bugs downstream.
stories.task: I had to stabilize the foundation before chasing optimizations.
stories.action: I tightened the grammar, added regression tests for the nasty edge cases, and only then layered optimizations once the baseline was green.
stories.result: After that, other people could actually build on it without fighting ghosts.

stories.title: gRPC service layer + tests
stories.situation: The podcast side was moving toward Java services talking gRPC with Protocol Buffers, and honestly if ingestion data was wrong, it wasn’t just a UI bug—it hit creators and listeners downstream.
stories.task: My job was to help make that pipeline accurate for internal services feeding ingestion and distribution—think tens of thousands of monthly listeners—while keeping the contracts something we could actually test.
stories.action: I worked in Java and gRPC, tightened protobuf definitions, and added real end-to-end coverage across the RPC boundary—not only happy-path unit tests. I also validated against real ingestion flows so we weren’t fooling ourselves.
stories.result: We roughly doubled measured accuracy on the ingestion path, which made it safer for other teams to build on top of those services.

question: Describe a time when you led a team. What was the outcome?

stories.title: NSBE Gen-Sec + partnerships
stories.situation: I’m Gen-Sec for NSBE at Grambling starting January 2025. After the pandemic dip, people wanted the chapter to feel technically serious—not just another social club.
stories.task: I needed to rebuild energy and land sponsors in a way that actually helped CS students.
stories.action: So I got practical about outreach, aligned officers around what members were asking for, and when we talked to companies I made sure we could point to real impact—not vibes.
stories.result: We grew past 300 members and brought in about three thousand dollars in technical partnerships.

stories.title: MedDiagnose stack + pilot
stories.situation: MedDiagnose was a full-stack stroke project—Python and TypeScript, FastAPI, TensorFlow, React—with real-time monitoring, NIHSS scoring, RBAC, and tPA eligibility checks. The goal was to cut delays, not to win a Kaggle trophy.
stories.task: I needed something we could honestly take into a Louisiana hospital pilot—not a demo that oversold the ML.
stories.action: I worked across FastAPI, the React clinical UI, and the model side. The Random Forest stroke-risk score landed around 45% accuracy, so I was upfront about that and focused the story on workflow wins for neurologists.
stories.result: We ran a hospital pilot that helped with early detection and cut time-to-treatment by about 35%, which was the outcome clinicians actually cared about.

stories.title: VisualizeMyAlgorithm scale
stories.situation: I built VisualizeMyAlgorithm in React—think pathfinding, sorting, even neural-net style demos—and over a thousand learners have used it.
stories.task: I wanted it to feel fast and actually teach, not just look cool.
stories.action: I built modular visualization components—Dijkstra, A*, Minimax, that kind of thing—plus the state management and rendering pipeline so you could step through execution instead of watching a GIF.
stories.result: People said they understood concepts faster—roughly 40% faster in the informal timing we did—and the thing could handle a real classroom without the UI freezing.

question: Tell me about a time you worked well under pressure.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: I’m in ColorStack, and their hackathons pull serious competition—Winter 2024 had on the order of a thousand people worldwide.
stories.task: We had to ship something demoable on a tight clock.
stories.action: Our team cut scope to an MVP early, split frontend and backend ownership clearly, and rehearsed the demo so we weren’t doing live surgery on stage.
stories.result: We won Winter 2024, which was a good proof that I can execute when time is not on your side.

stories.title: RBAC, logging, and a11y on RPC
stories.situation: Creators and admins were touching sensitive flows, and some RPC endpoints really couldn’t behave the same for both. We also needed enough logging and accessibility coverage that we weren’t flying blind—or shipping something only usable for one narrow path.
stories.task: I needed to harden the product without blocking the main feature work.
stories.action: So I added interaction logging on the critical flows, ran accessibility testing on the key UI paths, and put role-based access control on the sensitive gRPC endpoints so “creator vs admin” wasn’t ambiguous.
stories.result: It cut down on privilege mix-ups, made audits easier, and honestly made cross-team collaboration less scary because expectations were explicit.

stories.situation: Algorithm viz is state-heavy. If you’re not careful in React, you rerender yourself into the ground.
stories.task: I needed smooth interactions even on bigger graphs and mid-tier laptops.
stories.action: So I batched updates per tick, memoized the expensive stuff, and capped work per frame so we weren’t dropping frames during a lecture.

question: Describe a time you received tough or critical feedback.

stories.title: Service-layer maintainability
stories.situation: At Geaux, my first gRPC/protobuf slice passed tests, but teammates told me it was hard to extend—names and module boundaries didn’t make ownership obvious.
stories.task: I needed to take the feedback seriously and make the next changes easier for everyone, not just me.
stories.action: I cleaned up how we packaged protobufs, added contract tests right at the RPC edge, and wrote down what errors actually mean for callers.
stories.result: Reviews got less painful, and the next features landed without those messy integration surprises.

stories.title: ML calibration reality check
stories.situation: Mentors basically said, “You can’t headline 45% accuracy like it’s a flex—people will misunderstand what the model can do.”
stories.task: I needed to tell the story in a way clinicians and leadership could use, not misread.
stories.action: I moved the narrative toward calibration, clearer language about failure modes, and workflow numbers like time-to-treatment instead of a big accuracy banner.
stories.result: People actually used the dashboard. Clinicians leaned in more because it felt honest.

stories.title: Compiler scope creep
stories.situation: On my mini compiler someone called me out for optimizing before the parser was solid—classic premature optimization, and they were right.
stories.task: I had to reorder the work: correctness first, speed second.
stories.action: I paused the fancy IR passes until lexer and parser tests covered the weird tokens, then brought optimizations back with real benchmarks.
stories.result: The compiler got stable, and the feedback stopped being an issue because the foundation was there.

question: Describe a time when you had to give someone difficult feedback. How did you handle it?

stories.title: Peer tests at Hubtel
stories.situation: A teammate wanted to merge a Spring change, but integration coverage around a payment edge case was thin—and I’d already seen that path fail in staging.
stories.task: I needed to flag it without making it personal.
stories.action: I reproduced it with a small Postman sequence, dropped the logs in chat, and suggested a focused JUnit setup—maybe Testcontainers—so we weren’t debating vibes. I framed it as “we both own quality,” not “you messed up.”
stories.result: They added tests, we merged, and I slept better.

stories.title: NSBE officer workload
stories.situation: One officer kept promising huge turnout numbers, and sponsors and volunteers were getting burned when reality didn’t match.
stories.task: I needed to give direct feedback, but do it privately and with respect.
stories.action: I pulled real attendance trends, compared them to what we’d promised, and suggested smaller events we could actually fill. I also pointed out what they were great at—marketing—so it wasn’t “you’re bad,” it was “let’s align promises.”
stories.result: Next quarter we hit our targets without exhausting people.

stories.title: Hackathon scope
stories.situation: During ColorStack Winter someone wanted a live ML API wired up, and with twelve hours left that was not going to be stable.
stories.task: I needed a Plan B fast.
stories.action: I suggested we mock the boundary, record outputs, and demo against that so judges saw real behavior without a live wire that could 500 mid-pitch.
stories.result: We shipped on time and the story was still technically honest.

stories.result: We grew past 300 members and brought in about three thousand dollars in technical partnerships.

question: Tell me about a time when you had to deal with a significant change at work. How did you adapt?

stories.title: gRPC service layer + tests
stories.situation: The podcast side was moving toward Java services talking gRPC with Protocol Buffers, and honestly if ingestion data was wrong, it wasn’t just a UI bug—it hit creators and listeners downstream.
stories.task: My job was to help make that pipeline accurate for internal services feeding ingestion and distribution—think tens of thousands of monthly listeners—while keeping the contracts something we could actually test.
stories.action: I worked in Java and gRPC, tightened protobuf definitions, and added real end-to-end coverage across the RPC boundary—not only happy-path unit tests. I also validated against real ingestion flows so we weren’t fooling ourselves.
stories.result: We roughly doubled measured accuracy on the ingestion path, which made it safer for other teams to build on top of those services.

stories.title: Kafka/MQTT/Postgres pipeline
stories.situation: I interned at Hubtel in Ghana in 2023. The business was super transaction-heavy, so system events were noisy and downtime was real money—and real trust.
stories.task: I needed to help the team catch problems faster without destabilizing the payment workflows everyone depended on.
stories.action: I worked on a real-time pipeline with Kafka Connect, MQTT, and Postgres—basically following events from broker to sink, using integration tests and metrics when something looked off instead of guessing.
stories.result: We got downtime detection time down by something like 40%, and payments felt more available because we weren’t always firefighting blind.

stories.title: Full-stack reliability
stories.situation: When you’re monitoring patients in real time, a React state bug can look like a clinical emergency. And if RBAC is wrong, you’re not just shipping a bug—you’re risking data.
stories.task: I needed the system to stay stable under load and stay compliant by design.
stories.action: So I focused on RBAC end to end, clearer API errors, and making the monitoring views render fast enough that people would actually use them.
stories.result: The pilot held up, and the hospital team felt like access control matched how they work.

question: Describe a situation where you saw a problem and took the initiative to correct it rather than waiting for someone else.

stories.title: RBAC, logging, and a11y on RPC
stories.situation: Creators and admins were touching sensitive flows, and some RPC endpoints really couldn’t behave the same for both. We also needed enough logging and accessibility coverage that we weren’t flying blind—or shipping something only usable for one narrow path.
stories.task: I needed to harden the product without blocking the main feature work.
stories.action: So I added interaction logging on the critical flows, ran accessibility testing on the key UI paths, and put role-based access control on the sensitive gRPC endpoints so “creator vs admin” wasn’t ambiguous.
stories.result: It cut down on privilege mix-ups, made audits easier, and honestly made cross-team collaboration less scary because expectations were explicit.

stories.title: Spring Boot test expansion
stories.situation: A lot of the scary failures weren’t “unit test red”—they were integration-shaped: database, messaging, HTTP handoffs. That’s normal for payment-adjacent services.
stories.task: I needed to raise confidence in the backend paths that touched money.
stories.action: I wrote more than twenty Spring Boot tests—JUnit plus Postman collections—and I focused on transaction boundaries and the ways things actually break, not just bumping coverage for the number.
stories.result: In the areas we targeted, reliability improved a ton—on the order of 80%—and we caught regressions before they hit prod way more often.

stories.title: VisualizeMyAlgorithm scale
stories.situation: I built VisualizeMyAlgorithm in React—think pathfinding, sorting, even neural-net style demos—and over a thousand learners have used it.
stories.task: I wanted it to feel fast and actually teach, not just look cool.
stories.action: I built modular visualization components—Dijkstra, A*, Minimax, that kind of thing—plus the state management and rendering pipeline so you could step through execution instead of watching a GIF.
stories.result: People said they understood concepts faster—roughly 40% faster in the informal timing we did—and the thing could handle a real classroom without the UI freezing.

question: Describe a time when there was a conflict within your team. How did you help resolve it? Did you do anything to prevent it in future?

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation
stories.situation: So at Geaux I was an Associate SWE Intern, remote, summer 2025. Engineering and content both cared about “who changed what” for podcasts and media, but the truth was spread across a bunch of backend event sources that didn’t always line up.
stories.task: I needed to ship a Change History view in TypeScript and SCSS that actually helped people trace edits—without trying to boil the ocean on v1.
stories.action: What I did was get both sides in one room, stack-rank the event sources by how much traceability we’d get per unit of work, then ship in phases. I surfaced the highest-value protobuf-backed events first and tightened integration tests on the read path so we weren’t guessing in prod.
stories.result: We ended up with much clearer traceability end to end, and because we phased it, releases stayed predictable instead of turning into a never-ending scope creep thing.

stories.title: Cross-officer conflict
stories.situation: Officers argued about hackathons vs corporate events vs K-12 outreach. Everyone had a pet priority.
stories.task: I needed to turn that into a plan we could execute instead of debating forever.
stories.action: I ran quick surveys, looked at what people actually showed up for, and mapped sponsor commitments to specific events so we weren’t overpromising.
stories.result: Once priorities were evidence-based, execution got easier and the drama cooled down.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: I’m in ColorStack, and their hackathons pull serious competition—Winter 2024 had on the order of a thousand people worldwide.
stories.task: We had to ship something demoable on a tight clock.
stories.action: Our team cut scope to an MVP early, split frontend and backend ownership clearly, and rehearsed the demo so we weren’t doing live surgery on stage.
stories.result: We won Winter 2024, which was a good proof that I can execute when time is not on your side.

question: Describe a time when you took a big risk and it failed.

stories.title: ML + clinical translation
stories.situation: Clinicians don’t think in “model accuracy” the way engineers do—they care about when the score is misleading, when to ignore it, and how it fits into a busy shift.
stories.task: I needed the ML to be helpful without pretending it replaces judgment.
stories.action: I translated what the model was doing into plain language in the UI, documented uncertainty, and made the human decision points obvious instead of hidden.
stories.result: That kept trust. Automation still helped with routing, alerts, and eligibility prep—just in a way people could defend in a real hospital.

stories.title: Mini-compiler engineering
stories.situation: I built a mini compiler in C, C++, Python, and Yacc—lexing, parsing, IR, optimizations—basically a teaching tool for people learning how compilers work.
stories.task: I needed it to be correct enough that people trusted it, but still easy to extend.
stories.action: I implemented the lexer and parser, IR generation, constant folding, dead-code elimination, and decent error messages when things broke.
stories.result: Fifty-plus developers ended up using it, and folks said debugging time dropped by around 35% compared to the usual “edit and pray” homework loop.

question: How would you design or test a product to make sure it's diverse and inclusive to all users?

stories.title: RBAC + a11y baseline
stories.situation: For me, inclusion isn’t a sticker you slap on at the end. If creators and admins see different things, permissions and keyboard paths have to work for both—or you’ve built a product that only works for one persona.
stories.task: I try to bake that into acceptance criteria up front.
stories.action: At Geaux I paired accessibility checks with RBAC on the RPC-backed UI so we didn’t ship a flow that “worked for me” but broke for a restricted role.
stories.result: We caught a few ugly gaps before real users did.

question: How do you stay up-to-date with the latest technological advancements?

stories.title: Hands-on with real stacks
stories.situation: I don’t learn much from reading random blogs if I’m not shipping with the tool. I’ll follow release notes for stuff I actually touch—gRPC and protobuf, Kafka, React, TensorFlow when I’m in that world.
stories.task: If something sounds interesting, I want to try it in a branch, not just bookmark it.
stories.action: I’ll spike a change in VisualizeMyAlgorithm or my compiler project, benchmark it, and only then adopt it.
stories.result: That’s how I avoid chasing hype that doesn’t survive real code.

stories.title: Papers + practice
stories.situation: With ML, a Kaggle score doesn’t mean it’ll survive a hospital pilot. Data shifts, labels are messy, and “accuracy” can lie.
stories.task: I try to stay grounded in how models fail, not just how they win.
stories.action: MedDiagnose forced me to care about drift and evaluation beyond one headline number.
stories.result: So when I read new architectures, I’m asking “where does this break in production?” not “is it trendy?”

stories.title: Community signal
stories.situation: NSBE and ColorStack put me around people who are grinding the same interviews and stacks I am.
stories.task: It’s a fast feedback loop on what’s actually being asked.
stories.action: I swap system-design notes and “what just showed up in interviews” stories with peers who’ve been through tough loops.
stories.result: It keeps my study list aligned with what employers really test.

question: Give an example of a time you had to debug a challenging technical issue.

stories.title: gRPC contract mismatch
stories.situation: We renamed a protobuf field and suddenly the service started 500ing—classic client and server on different generated stubs.
stories.task: I needed to find the mismatch without thrashing production.
stories.action: I walked the `.proto` history, pinned versions in CI so codegen couldn’t drift silently, and added a contract test that fails if someone ships stale stubs.
stories.result: After that, deploys got boring in the best way—fewer surprise outages.

question: Why are you interested in working at [company name]?

stories.situation: I get excited when I can point to the impact—latency, availability, accuracy, whatever the real KPI is. If it’s just shipping widgets with no measurement, I lose steam.
stories.task: I’m looking for a place where the work lines up with the kind of backend, data, and full-stack problems I’ve already wrestled with.
stories.action: Geaux gave me gRPC and protobuf and RBAC. Hubtel gave me Kafka, MQTT, Postgres, Firebase. MedDiagnose gave me clinical ML with React on top. That’s the flavor of depth I want more of.
stories.result: I’m basically trying to find the next team where the bar is high and the problems are real.

stories.title: Growth vector
stories.situation: I learn fastest when the system fights back—schema changes, idempotency, weird failure modes—not toy CRUD tutorials.
stories.task: I want a team with real code review and real expectations, not theater.
stories.action: I’ve chased internships and projects that pushed me into distributed systems, messy ML in the real world, and low-level compiler-style thinking on purpose.
stories.result: Long term I want deeper ownership on services and cleaner collaboration across teams—not just closing tickets in a silo.

stories.title: Mission + craft
stories.situation: Healthcare and fintech both taught me that “works on my laptop” is not the flex people think it is. Auditability and correctness actually matter.
stories.task: I’m drawn to places that invest in testing and observability, and that care about inclusive product design—not as a buzzword, but as behavior.
stories.action: That lines up with how I approached RBAC, logging, accessibility checks, and honest ML framing on MedDiagnose.
stories.result: That’s the kind of environment where I do my best work.

question: Assume you are given a task to design a system. How would you do it? How would you resolve ambiguity?

stories.title: Clarify SLOs and boundaries
stories.situation: When a design task feels fuzzy, it’s usually because nobody said what “good” means—latency, durability, compliance, that kind of thing.
stories.task: I start from the user journey and what breaks if we’re wrong.
stories.action: I sketch data flows, call out trust boundaries like RBAC and PII, and only then pick patterns—REST, gRPC, events—based on coupling and scale. It’s similar to how Geaux and Hubtel split responsibilities.

question: What is the biggest technical challenge you have worked on?

stories.title: Kafka/MQTT/Postgres pipeline
stories.situation: I interned at Hubtel in Ghana in 2023. The business was super transaction-heavy, so system events were noisy and downtime was real money—and real trust.
stories.task: I needed to help the team catch problems faster without destabilizing the payment workflows everyone depended on.
stories.action: I worked on a real-time pipeline with Kafka Connect, MQTT, and Postgres—basically following events from broker to sink, using integration tests and metrics when something looked off instead of guessing.
stories.result: We got downtime detection time down by something like 40%, and payments felt more available because we weren’t always firefighting blind.

stories.title: MedDiagnose stack + pilot
stories.situation: MedDiagnose was a full-stack stroke project—Python and TypeScript, FastAPI, TensorFlow, React—with real-time monitoring, NIHSS scoring, RBAC, and tPA eligibility checks. The goal was to cut delays, not to win a Kaggle trophy.

stories.title: gRPC service layer + tests
stories.situation: The podcast side was moving toward Java services talking gRPC with Protocol Buffers, and honestly if ingestion data was wrong, it wasn’t just a UI bug—it hit creators and listeners downstream.
stories.task: My job was to help make that pipeline accurate for internal services feeding ingestion and distribution—think tens of thousands of monthly listeners—while keeping the contracts something we could actually test.

question: Why do you want to change your current company?

stories.title: Impact + systems
stories.situation: I get excited when I can point to the impact—latency, availability, accuracy, whatever the real KPI is. If it’s just shipping widgets with no measurement, I lose steam.
stories.task: I’m looking for a place where the work lines up with the kind of backend, data, and full-stack problems I’ve already wrestled with.
stories.action: Geaux gave me gRPC and protobuf and RBAC. Hubtel gave me Kafka, MQTT, Postgres, Firebase. MedDiagnose gave me clinical ML with React on top. That’s the flavor of depth I want more of.
stories.result: I’m basically trying to find the next team where the bar is high and the problems are real.

stories.title: Growth vector
stories.situation: I learn fastest when the system fights back—schema changes, idempotency, weird failure modes—not toy CRUD tutorials.
stories.task: I want a team with real code review and real expectations, not theater.
stories.action: I’ve chased internships and projects that pushed me into distributed systems, messy ML in the real world, and low-level compiler-style thinking on purpose.
stories.result: Long term I want deeper ownership on services and cleaner collaboration across teams—not just closing tickets in a silo.

stories.title: Mission + craft
stories.situation: Healthcare and fintech both taught me that “works on my laptop” is not the flex people think it is. Auditability and correctness actually matter.
stories.task: I’m drawn to places that invest in testing and observability, and that care about inclusive product design—not as a buzzword, but as behavior.
stories.action: That lines up with how I approached RBAC, logging, accessibility checks, and honest ML framing on MedDiagnose.
stories.result: That’s the kind of environment where I do my best work.

question: Tell me a time when you had a different opinion than the rest of the team. How did you handle it?

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation
stories.situation: So at Geaux I was an Associate SWE Intern, remote, summer 2025. Engineering and content both cared about “who changed what” for podcasts and media, but the truth was spread across a bunch of backend event sources that didn’t always line up.
stories.task: I needed to ship a Change History view in TypeScript and SCSS that actually helped people trace edits—without trying to boil the ocean on v1.
stories.action: What I did was get both sides in one room, stack-rank the event sources by how much traceability we’d get per unit of work, then ship in phases. I surfaced the highest-value protobuf-backed events first and tightened integration tests on the read path so we weren’t guessing in prod.
stories.result: We ended up with much clearer traceability end to end, and because we phased it, releases stayed predictable instead of turning into a never-ending scope creep thing.

stories.title: ML + clinical translation
stories.situation: Clinicians don’t think in “model accuracy” the way engineers do—they care about when the score is misleading, when to ignore it, and how it fits into a busy shift.
stories.task: I needed the ML to be helpful without pretending it replaces judgment.
stories.action: I translated what the model was doing into plain language in the UI, documented uncertainty, and made the human decision points obvious instead of hidden.
stories.result: That kept trust. Automation still helped with routing, alerts, and eligibility prep—just in a way people could defend in a real hospital.

question: Describe a time when you needed to motivate a group of individuals or encourage collaboration during a particular project.

question: What do you do to enhance your technical knowledge apart from your project work?

stories.title: Structured fundamentals
stories.situation: School gives me the vocabulary—OS, DSA—and internships give me the bruises. When I see backpressure in Kafka or weird blocking in gRPC, I can map it to something I read in class.
stories.task: I don’t want theory and practice to live in separate worlds.
stories.action: When I hit a failure mode I don’t understand, I’ll go back to the core material instead of only Stack Overflowing.
stories.result: It makes new stacks feel less like magic and more like patterns.

stories.title: Open source reading
stories.situation: I read real server code and gRPC examples from actual repos—not just hello-world tutorials.
stories.task: I’m trying to pick up idioms, not copy-paste.
stories.action: I’ll diff how upstream handles errors and versioning, then try a tiny repro in a branch before I touch prod services.
stories.result: My Geaux reviews got easier because I could say “this is how mature codebases do it” instead of inventing something one-off.

stories.title: Mentor feedback
stories.situation: Mentors in ColorStack and NSBE are blunt in a good way—resume depth, tests, how I talk in interviews.
stories.task: When they flag something, I treat it like a bug ticket.
stories.action: I turn it into drills: extra Spring/JUnit cases, tighter demo scripts, resume bullets that actually tie to metrics.
stories.result: Once I did that consistently, interviews felt less random.

stories.title: RBAC, logging, and a11y on RPC
stories.situation: Creators and admins were touching sensitive flows, and some RPC endpoints really couldn’t behave the same for both. We also needed enough logging and accessibility coverage that we weren’t flying blind—or shipping something only usable for one narrow path.
stories.task: I needed to harden the product without blocking the main feature work.
stories.action: So I added interaction logging on the critical flows, ran accessibility testing on the key UI paths, and put role-based access control on the sensitive gRPC endpoints so “creator vs admin” wasn’t ambiguous.
stories.result: It cut down on privilege mix-ups, made audits easier, and honestly made cross-team collaboration less scary because expectations were explicit.

stories.result: After that we stopped stepping on each other.

stories.situation: A lot of the scary failures weren’t “unit test red”—they were integration-shaped: database, messaging, HTTP handoffs. That’s normal for payment-adjacent services.
stories.task: I needed to raise confidence in the backend paths that touched money.
stories.action: I wrote more than twenty Spring Boot tests—JUnit plus Postman collections—and I focused on transaction boundaries and the ways things actually break, not just bumping coverage for the number.
stories.result: In the areas we targeted, reliability improved a ton—on the order of 80%—and we caught regressions before they hit prod way more often.

stories.result: Auth lived in one spot, and reviews got easier because we weren’t hunting scattered guards.

question: Tell me a time when you linked two or more problems together and identified an underlying issue.

stories.title: Downtime symptoms vs root cause
stories.situation: Alerts made it look like Postgres was the villain, but when I looked closer, MQTT was backing off under bursts and everything downstream screamed.
stories.task: I needed to find the real choke point instead of restarting the database.
stories.action: I lined up Kafka lag, MQTT publish rates, and Postgres stalls, then tuned batching and consumer parallelism where it actually mattered.
stories.result: Mean time to detect got way better—about 40%—because we fixed upstream, not just the symptom.

stories.title: Change History: event consolidation
stories.situation: So at Geaux I was an Associate SWE Intern, remote, summer 2025. Engineering and content both cared about “who changed what” for podcasts and media, but the truth was spread across a bunch of backend event sources that didn’t always line up.
stories.task: I needed to ship a Change History view in TypeScript and SCSS that actually helped people trace edits—without trying to boil the ocean on v1.
stories.action: What I did was get both sides in one room, stack-rank the event sources by how much traceability we’d get per unit of work, then ship in phases. I surfaced the highest-value protobuf-backed events first and tightened integration tests on the read path so we weren’t guessing in prod.
stories.result: We ended up with much clearer traceability end to end, and because we phased it, releases stayed predictable instead of turning into a never-ending scope creep thing.

stories.title: State + performance
stories.situation: Algorithm viz is state-heavy. If you’re not careful in React, you rerender yourself into the ground.
stories.task: I needed smooth interactions even on bigger graphs and mid-tier laptops.
stories.action: So I batched updates per tick, memoized the expensive stuff, and capped work per frame so we weren’t dropping frames during a lecture.
stories.result: That’s why professors could actually adopt it without handing students a gaming laptop.

question: Tell me about a time you made a decision to sacrifice short-term gain for a longer-term goal.

stories.title: Tests before features
stories.situation: I could’ve shipped a flashy VisualizeMyAlgorithm update for quick attention, but the state management was messy and the UI stuttered on big graphs.
stories.task: I chose boring refactors over short-term hype.
stories.action: I batched updates per tick, memoized the expensive bits, and added simple perf checks in CI before new viz work.
stories.result: Demos for Dijkstra, A*, Minimax got smoother, and I wasn’t debugging “why did the page freeze?” at midnight.

stories.title: ML + clinical translation
stories.situation: Clinicians don’t think in “model accuracy” the way engineers do—they care about when the score is misleading, when to ignore it, and how it fits into a busy shift.
stories.task: I needed the ML to be helpful without pretending it replaces judgment.
stories.action: I translated what the model was doing into plain language in the UI, documented uncertainty, and made the human decision points obvious instead of hidden.
stories.result: That kept trust. Automation still helped with routing, alerts, and eligibility prep—just in a way people could defend in a real hospital.

stories.result: We ended up with much clearer traceability end to end, and because we phased it, releases stayed predictable instead of turning into a never-ending scope creep thing.

question: How would you respond if you were the last member of the team in the office on a Friday afternoon and the product owner asks you to develop and deploy a change to production?

stories.title: Production change discipline
stories.situation: Friday afternoon “we need this in prod now” energy is real, but a lot of the time it’s urgency without severity.
stories.task: My instinct is to protect users and the team from a sloppy deploy.
stories.action: I’d ask what’s actually broken, who’s affected, whether we can roll back, and whether on-call is around. If it’s fuzzy, I’d rather ship a tiny reversible change behind a flag—or wait until Monday with a clear incident write-up—than gamble.
stories.result: That “slow down and get the facts first” approach is what kept releases sane for me at Hubtel and Geaux.

stories.title: MedDiagnose lesson: trust
stories.situation: With MedDiagnose, not everyone loved the ML numbers at first—and that’s fair. Clinical folks deserve honesty.
stories.task: I needed to be straight about risk instead of selling a fairy tale.
stories.action: I was upfront that the RF model was around 45% accurate on that risk signal, and I paired that with the workflow outcome we could prove—like faster time-to-treatment, roughly 35% improvement in the pilot.
stories.result: People respected that more than hype. Trust went up, and we didn’t pretend the model was magic.

stories.title: Evidence over noise
stories.situation: In event systems, the symptom often shows up downstream when the real issue is upstream—serialization, backpressure, something like that.
stories.task: I try not to patch the first thing that looks red.
stories.action: At Hubtel I walked Kafka to MQTT to Postgres with logs and tests before touching the “obvious” hot path. That stopped repeat incidents where we kept treating the wrong layer.
stories.result: That’s the same discipline I’d bring to a Friday prod question—I’m going to trace it, not guess.

question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

stories.title: Staff-shaped ownership
stories.situation: In five years I want to be the engineer who owns real services end to end—API design, reliability, working with product—and can point to metrics that prove it.
stories.task: I don’t want depth only in a corner nobody sees.
stories.action: Geaux, Hubtel, and MedDiagnose already mix backend, data, and user-facing surfaces; I want that at larger scale.
stories.result: That means mentoring people, sharper architecture, and fewer 3 a.m. fires.

stories.title: Growth vector
stories.situation: I learn fastest when the system fights back—schema changes, idempotency, weird failure modes—not toy CRUD tutorials.
stories.task: I want a team with real code review and real expectations, not theater.
stories.action: I’ve chased internships and projects that pushed me into distributed systems, messy ML in the real world, and low-level compiler-style thinking on purpose.
stories.result: Long term I want deeper ownership on services and cleaner collaboration across teams—not just closing tickets in a silo.

stories.title: Impact + systems
stories.situation: I get excited when I can point to the impact—latency, availability, accuracy, whatever the real KPI is. If it’s just shipping widgets with no measurement, I lose steam.
stories.task: I’m looking for a place where the work lines up with the kind of backend, data, and full-stack problems I’ve already wrestled with.
stories.action: Geaux gave me gRPC and protobuf and RBAC. Hubtel gave me Kafka, MQTT, Postgres, Firebase. MedDiagnose gave me clinical ML with React on top. That’s the flavor of depth I want more of.
stories.result: I’m basically trying to find the next team where the bar is high and the problems are real.

question: What does your best day of work look like?

stories.title: Deep work + shipping
stories.situation: A great day for me is pretty simple: CI is green on something I care about, I get a focused block to profile or trace a real issue, and I still have time to help someone with tests or design.
stories.task: I like building alone and winning with the team—not one or the other.
stories.action: That’s what my best days at Geaux and Hubtel felt like.
stories.result: I go to bed tired but knowing something moved—latency, coverage, or a user unblocked.

stories.title: Impact + systems
stories.situation: I get excited when I can point to the impact—latency, availability, accuracy, whatever the real KPI is. If it’s just shipping widgets with no measurement, I lose steam.
stories.task: I’m looking for a place where the work lines up with the kind of backend, data, and full-stack problems I’ve already wrestled with.
stories.action: Geaux gave me gRPC and protobuf and RBAC. Hubtel gave me Kafka, MQTT, Postgres, Firebase. MedDiagnose gave me clinical ML with React on top. That’s the flavor of depth I want more of.
stories.result: I’m basically trying to find the next team where the bar is high and the problems are real.

stories.title: Pedagogy + engineering
stories.situation: The whole point was learning, not flashy graphics for their own sake.
stories.task: I wanted every animation tied to something a student could verify—like “this is the frontier” or “this edge just relaxed.”
stories.action: I synced what you saw on screen with the algorithm’s real invariants so people could connect the code to the motion.
stories.result: It felt less like magic, and students actually retained the fundamentals.

question: What words would your colleagues use to describe you?

stories.title: Rigorous tester
stories.situation: At Hubtel, people knew I’d push back if money-moving flows didn’t have real integration tests.
stories.task: I’d rather slow a merge than ship blind.
stories.action: JUnit and Postman weren’t glamorous, but they ended arguments.
stories.result: I hear “thorough” a lot in feedback—I will take it.

stories.title: Systems thinker
stories.situation: At Geaux, teammates asked me to trace protobuf contracts across services when things got fuzzy.
stories.task: I like drawing the system before I code.
stories.action: I sketch flows, retries, idempotency—what breaks for creators vs admins—so we don’t discover it in prod.
stories.result: People say I make the scary parts smaller before they blow up.

stories.title: Mission-driven
stories.situation: In NSBE and ColorStack I think people would say I show up with a plan—not just energy.
stories.task: I care about logistics, not only speeches.
stories.action: RSVP targets, sponsor deliverables, workshops that match what interviews actually ask.
stories.result: We hit attendance goals and members actually land opportunities—that’s the point.

question: What is something that you had to push for in your previous projects?

stories.title: RBAC, logging, and a11y on RPC
stories.situation: Creators and admins were touching sensitive flows, and some RPC endpoints really couldn’t behave the same for both. We also needed enough logging and accessibility coverage that we weren’t flying blind—or shipping something only usable for one narrow path.
stories.task: I needed to harden the product without blocking the main feature work.
stories.action: So I added interaction logging on the critical flows, ran accessibility testing on the key UI paths, and put role-based access control on the sensitive gRPC endpoints so “creator vs admin” wasn’t ambiguous.
stories.result: It cut down on privilege mix-ups, made audits easier, and honestly made cross-team collaboration less scary because expectations were explicit.

stories.title: Spring Boot test expansion
stories.situation: A lot of the scary failures weren’t “unit test red”—they were integration-shaped: database, messaging, HTTP handoffs. That’s normal for payment-adjacent services.
stories.task: I needed to raise confidence in the backend paths that touched money.
stories.action: I wrote more than twenty Spring Boot tests—JUnit plus Postman collections—and I focused on transaction boundaries and the ways things actually break, not just bumping coverage for the number.
stories.result: In the areas we targeted, reliability improved a ton—on the order of 80%—and we caught regressions before they hit prod way more often.

stories.title: VisualizeMyAlgorithm scale
stories.situation: I built VisualizeMyAlgorithm in React—think pathfinding, sorting, even neural-net style demos—and over a thousand learners have used it.
stories.task: I wanted it to feel fast and actually teach, not just look cool.
stories.action: I built modular visualization components—Dijkstra, A*, Minimax, that kind of thing—plus the state management and rendering pipeline so you could step through execution instead of watching a GIF.
stories.result: People said they understood concepts faster—roughly 40% faster in the informal timing we did—and the thing could handle a real classroom without the UI freezing.

question: What would you hope to achieve in the first six months after being hired?

stories.title: Ramp + ownership
stories.situation: In the first month I want to ship small, real fixes so I learn your build, deploy, and on-call reality. By two or three months I want to own a slice with clear SLOs. By six months I’d love to lead something cross-team—maybe observability or tightening tests around a pipeline.
stories.task: I’m not trying to just close tickets; I want impact I can measure.
stories.action: That’s similar to how I ramped at Geaux on gRPC and protobuf—I started narrow and earned scope.
stories.result: If I can point to reliability or latency wins, that’s a good six months.

stories.title: Relationships
stories.situation: Early on I’d map who matters—security, product, support—and agree how we communicate when things go wrong.
stories.task: It saves drama later.
stories.action: On MedDiagnose I did a lighter version of that with clinical staff: short updates, honest timelines.
stories.result: When something breaks, people already trust you because you didn’t ghost them in the calm times.

stories.title: Testing culture
stories.situation: I want to leave RPC and pipeline tests stronger than I found them—especially where bugs are expensive.
stories.task: That’s how you pay the next engineer forward.
stories.action: I’d spend real time on flaky tests and contract tests in the first quarter so velocity compounds instead of decaying.
stories.result: The next hire shouldn’t inherit mystery failures.

question: If this were your first annual review, what would I be telling you right now?

stories.title: Ship + measure
stories.situation: If this were my first review, you’d probably say: you shipped real stuff across TypeScript, Java and gRPC, and data pipelines—and you could point to numbers: roughly 40% better downtime detection at Hubtel, big reliability gains where we invested tests, and about 35% faster time-to-treatment in the MedDiagnose pilot.
stories.task: That’s a strong start for where I am in my career.
stories.action: The growth edge is estimation and communication across teams—owning timelines without sandbagging or overpromising.
stories.result: Next step is bigger ownership with clearer SLOs and helping others on test design, not just my own code.

stories.title: NSBE Gen-Sec + partnerships
stories.situation: I’m Gen-Sec for NSBE at Grambling starting January 2025. After the pandemic dip, people wanted the chapter to feel technically serious—not just another social club.
stories.task: I needed to rebuild energy and land sponsors in a way that actually helped CS students.
stories.action: So I got practical about outreach, aligned officers around what members were asking for, and when we talked to companies I made sure we could point to real impact—not vibes.
stories.result: We grew past 300 members and brought in about three thousand dollars in technical partnerships.

stories.title: ColorStack hackathon win
stories.situation: I’m in ColorStack, and their hackathons pull serious competition—Winter 2024 had on the order of a thousand people worldwide.
stories.task: We had to ship something demoable on a tight clock.
stories.action: Our team cut scope to an MVP early, split frontend and backend ownership clearly, and rehearsed the demo so we weren’t doing live surgery on stage.
stories.result: We won Winter 2024, which was a good proof that I can execute when time is not on your side.

question: What is your biggest strength and area of growth?

stories.title: Strength: end-to-end debugging
stories.situation: I’m pretty calm when things break across protobuf, Kafka and MQTT, and the UI—I’ve done that at Hubtel and Geaux.
stories.task: Where I’m growing is knowing when to pull someone in earlier.
stories.action: I can over-investigate before I escalate; I’m working on time-boxing and giving leads a tight summary with what I’ve ruled out.
stories.result: They get signal sooner, and I still finish the fix—I’m not dropping the ball.

stories.title: Strength: responsible ML framing
stories.situation: MedDiagnose taught me to talk about ML next to workflow outcomes, not a single accuracy number.
stories.task: I want more depth in real MLOps—not just training notebooks.
stories.action: I’m reading more about drift monitoring and what retraining looks like when you’re not in a classroom.
stories.result: I’m closing that gap with side projects and coursework where I can.

stories.title: Strength: community leadership
stories.situation: NSBE is proof I can execute—membership growth, real sponsor dollars.
stories.task: I’m still learning to delegate so it doesn’t all ride on me.
stories.action: I’m trying to build other leaders, not only run events myself.
stories.result: The win is when programs keep working even when I’m slammed with school.

question: What aspects of your work are most often criticized?

stories.title: Over-engineering risk
stories.situation: I’ve been called out for reaching for abstractions too early—I get excited about clean design.
stories.task: I’m learning to prove the need with data first.
stories.action: Now I try the smallest change that meets the SLO, and only refactor when the pattern repeats.
stories.result: PRs move faster and people spend less time debating architecture in the abstract.

stories.title: Estimation optimism
stories.situation: Compiler and viz projects taught me pretty visuals can hide a week of integration pain.
stories.task: I’m getting better at padding estimates for unknowns.
stories.action: I slice work vertically so there’s something demoable sooner.
stories.result: Stakeholders get fewer “surprise, we need another sprint” moments.

stories.title: Async updates
stories.situation: Remote work means nobody sees you thinking—mentors told me I need to over-communicate status.
stories.task: I’m working on smaller, more frequent updates.
stories.action: Short standup notes or doc updates when something shifts.
stories.result: Managers stopped pinging me for “where are we?” because it’s already written down.

question: Tell me about a time you needed information from someone who wasn't responsive.

stories.title: Blocked on proto review
stories.situation: A senior engineer had my protobuf review sitting, and my Geaux feature was stuck behind it.
stories.task: I couldn’t spam them, but I couldn’t wait forever either.
stories.action: I sent a tight summary of the diff, listed exact questions, and offered two short slots. If it slipped past what we’d agreed, I escalated with facts—who was blocked downstream.
stories.result: The review landed, and they actually thanked me for making it easy to consume.

stories.title: Clinical feedback loop
stories.situation: During finals, neurologists weren’t answering MedDiagnose UX questions fast—and honestly, their time is more valuable than mine.
stories.task: I needed answers without being annoying.
stories.action: I batched questions, sent screenshots, and used short Loom videos so they could respond async.
stories.result: We got what we needed without burning bridges.

stories.title: Sponsor contact ghosting
stories.situation: For NSBE, a sponsor contact went quiet right when we needed a yes on budget so we could book travel.
stories.task: I needed an answer without sounding desperate.
stories.action: I sent a short email with two clear options—commit by this date or pause—and only looped a faculty advisor when it was fair.
stories.result: We got a decision in time to book or pivot to another sponsor without drama.

question: What frustrates you?

stories.title: Opaque incidents
stories.situation: Nothing frustrates me more than a postmortem that says “fixed” with no root cause and no follow-up tests.
stories.task: If we don’t learn, we’ll see the same fire again.
stories.action: At Hubtel I pushed for a real timeline, what actually failed, and what test would catch it next time—not just “we restarted it.”
stories.result: Once we fixed upstream backpressure instead of blaming Postgres, repeat noise dropped.

stories.title: Vanity metrics
stories.situation: Shipping features with no definition of success drives me nuts—if we can’t point to latency, errors, or a user outcome, what are we doing?
stories.task: I want work tied to something measurable.
stories.action: On MedDiagnose I’d rather talk about time-to-treatment than a pretty slide.
stories.result: Teams move faster when everyone agrees what “good” means.

stories.title: Gatekeeping
stories.situation: I get frustrated by communities that make beginners feel dumb—that’s a big reason I care about NSBE and ColorStack.
stories.task: Gatekeeping wastes talent.
stories.action: I help run intro workshops—DSA basics, git hygiene—so people have a real on-ramp.
stories.result: Retention goes up when people feel invited, not tested for coolness.

question: What is something 90% of people disagree with you about?

stories.title: Move slow to move fast
stories.situation: A lot of people want to hotfix on Friday and call it heroism. I’d rather ship a boring, tested change Monday.
stories.task: Tail risk matters more than adrenaline.
stories.action: Hubtel and MedDiagnose both taught me untested hotfixes come back as outages.
stories.result: I’d rather be the person who sleeps and keeps prod stable.

stories.title: Accuracy is not enough in ML
stories.situation: Hackathons love leaderboard scores. I care whether the model behaves in the real workflow—calibration, when it’s wrong, how clinicians actually use it.
stories.task: That’s not always a popular take in a 48-hour room.
stories.action: I push for human-in-the-loop UX and monitoring, not a vanity metric.
stories.result: Clinicians buy in when you’re honest; some hackathon judges don’t love it until they see pilot numbers.

stories.title: Community > grind culture
stories.situation: Some people brag about all-nighters. I don’t think that’s sustainable—or necessary.
stories.task: I’d rather ship consistently and stay healthy.
stories.action: I time-box, protect sleep during exams, and still shipped real projects at Geaux and on VisualizeMyAlgorithm.
stories.result: Dean’s List plus shipping beats burnout theater.

question: Tell me about a time you had to deliver difficult or bad news to someone.

stories.title: Model limits to stakeholders
stories.situation: I had to tell clinical partners the stroke risk model was sitting around 45% accuracy—that’s not what someone wants to hear if they wanted a headline “AI saves lives” story.
stories.task: Bad news lands better if you’re direct.
stories.action: I paired it with what we could prove on the workflow side—NIHSS, tPA checks, and about 35% faster time-to-treatment in the pilot.
stories.result: They could live with it as decision support, and the pilot kept going with clearer expectations.

stories.title: Sponsor shortfall
stories.situation: NSBE had to tell a sponsor we didn’t hit the attendance we promised for an event—that’s uncomfortable.
stories.task: We owned it instead of making excuses.
stories.action: We came back with a smaller workshop, guaranteed RSVPs, and a tighter plan.
stories.result: They stayed with us because we didn’t hide the miss.

stories.title: Demo failure prep
stories.situation: Six hours before a ColorStack deadline I told my team the live ML endpoint was too risky—we’d been fighting instability.
stories.task: I’d rather eat crow early than fail on stage.
stories.action: We stubbed inference with recorded outputs, kept the API contract stable, and rehearsed.
stories.result: We still won Winter 2024 out of roughly a thousand people—and the demo didn’t flake.
